skill.name: react-best-practices
skill.description: React and Next.js performance guidance for writing, reviewing, and refactoring applications with faster rendering, data fetching, and bundle loading patterns.
skill.summary: Apply Vercel-style React and Next.js performance rules during code generation and review.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many script, command, network, storage, and heuristic hits, but the reviewed evidence is markdown documentation with fenced React and Next.js examples. No prompt injection text, executable payload, credential exfiltration flow, or malicious intent was found. The skill is safe to publish with low residual risk from code examples that agents may copy into projects.

low_findings.title: Static Script Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Dynamic import and with-like token detections appear inside fenced markdown examples or bullet text about bundle optimization and immutable array APIs. They are not executable skill code and do not load attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown examples and plain list items. No executable script file or runtime import path controlled by the skill was found.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Backticks And Search Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by markdown inline code, fenced TypeScript examples, and grep examples for finding rule files. The skill does not define a script runner or ask the agent to execute untrusted commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation formatting and simple grep lookup guidance. I found no command construction, subprocess invocation, or shell execution helper.

low_findings.title: Network And Browser Storage Examples Are Benign Performance Guidance
low_findings.description: Fetch, hardcoded URL, localStorage, document.cookie, and keyboard key detections appear in examples about SWR deduplication, caching reads, and event listeners. They do not send secrets to external hosts or request unauthorized data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use local API paths, documentation URLs, and browser APIs for performance patterns. There is no evidence of credential collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Is A Co-occurrence False Positive
low_findings.description: The dangerous combination heuristic combines code examples, network examples, and key-related words from unrelated markdown sections. Reviewed context shows a React performance guide, not obfuscated behavior or credential access logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The correlated signals come from section impact labels and benign code snippets. I did not find prompt injection, obfuscation, credential harvesting, or a runnable payload.

content.value_statement: React and Next.js teams often repeat slow patterns in data fetching, rendering, and bundle loading. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete rules for faster code generation, review, and refactoring.
actual_capabilities: Guides React and Next.js code reviews for performance issues.
Suggests waterfall elimination patterns for async data fetching.
Recommends bundle splitting with dynamic imports and deferred libraries.
Provides server and client caching patterns, including React.cache and SWR.
Covers rendering and re-render reduction techniques for components.
Includes focused rule files for quick lookup by performance category.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not run benchmarks or profile applications.
Examples may need adaptation for project-specific framework versions.
It focuses on performance, not general accessibility or security review.
Some advanced patterns require human judgment before broad refactoring.

use_cases.title: Review a Next.js Pull Request
use_cases.description: Find performance regressions in data fetching, bundle size, and rendering before merge.

use_cases.title: Refactor Slow React Components
use_cases.description: Apply memoization, lazy initialization, and derived state patterns to reduce unnecessary work.

use_cases.title: Generate Faster Feature Code
use_cases.description: Use performance rules while creating new pages, server components, hooks, and client components.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Component Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React component for obvious performance issues. Focus on unnecessary renders, expensive initialization, and avoidable storage or DOM reads.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Data Fetching Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Next.js data fetching code for waterfalls. Recommend changes using parallel fetching, early promise starts, Suspense, or SWR where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Bundle Cost
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of page load performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this page for bundle size problems. Identify heavy imports, barrel imports, third-party libraries, and candidates for dynamic loading or preloading.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Performance Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized React and Next.js performance refactor plan for these files. Group findings by impact, explain tradeoffs, and list verification steps.

output_examples.input: A server-rendered page fetches user, billing, and project data in sequence.
output: Recommends starting independent requests before awaiting results.
Suggests preserving dependency order only where one result truly depends on another.

output_examples.input: A dashboard imports a chart library and analytics component in the root layout.
output: Suggests dynamic loading for heavy UI and deferred loading for non-critical analytics.
Adds notes about validating LCP and interaction timing after the change.

output_examples.input: A component reads localStorage and builds an index during every render.
output: Recommends lazy state initialization and in-memory caching where safe.
Calls out cache invalidation when browser storage can change externally.

best_practices: Start with high-impact rules: waterfalls, bundle size, and server-side data fetching.
Validate performance changes with profiling, bundle analysis, or user timing metrics.
Keep examples aligned with the project architecture before applying broad refactors.
anti_patterns: Applying micro-optimizations before fixing network waterfalls or large bundles.
Copying dynamic import patterns without checking server rendering requirements.
Caching browser storage or cookies without a clear invalidation strategy.

faq.question: Does this skill modify code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance that an AI coding agent can use while writing, reviewing, or refactoring code.

faq.question: Is it only for Next.js projects?
faq.answer: No. Many React rules apply broadly, but several server and routing examples are specific to Next.js.

faq.question: Can it replace performance profiling?
faq.answer: No. It helps identify likely issues, but profiling and measurement should confirm important changes.

faq.question: Does it cover accessibility?
faq.answer: Not directly. The skill focuses on React and Next.js performance patterns.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention script and network risk factors?
faq.answer: The documentation includes code examples with imports, fetch calls, and browser APIs. These are reviewed as non-executable examples.

faq.question: Which agents can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
